text: The robotic cholecystectomy is performed using the open book technique, with a medium-large clip applier visible on the right side of the screen, docked to arm four. The clip applier serves as both a liver retractor and gallbladder retractor, pushing the gallbladder and liver anteriorly without grasping the gallbladder. The gallbladder appears floppy, and the liver is pushed anteriorly, limiting further elevation of the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a clip applier to push the gallbladder and liver anteriorly, without using a grasper, and begins to open the peritoneum on the medial side of the gallbladder.

The surgeon adjusts the retraction using the shaft of the clip applier, which elevates the left lateral segment of the liver. This maneuver helps keep the liver out of the surgical field, particularly when accessing the cystic triangle. Care is taken to avoid excessive pulling that could cause the clip applier to dig into the liver.

The surgeon uses the clipper bar tip to detach the medial peritoneal attachments of the gallbladder fundus from the liver, resting the tip in the gallbladder fossa to minimize liver laceration risk. The Firefly imaging system is utilized to verify anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses the open book technique to roll the gallbladder away from the liver, gradually opening the peritoneum on the medial side to enhance exposure of the critical structures near the hepatocystic triangle.

The surgeon uses the open book technique to work anteriorly on the gallbladder, carefully preserving the cystic artery as it travels alongside the gallbladder and keeping it against the liver side. The procedure is performed well away from the hepatocystic triangle, ensuring safety while operating high up on the gallbladder.

The surgeon opens the medial side of the gallbladder, progressively pulling it away from the liver. This maneuver increases the distance between the gallbladder and the liver, enhancing safety during the operation. The gallbladder and infundibulum are visibly separated from the liver as the surgeon works up and down the medial side.
The surgeon identifies small branches of the cystic artery extending towards the liver and uses bipolar cautery to coagulate bleeding branches coming off the gallbladder.

The surgeon continues to open the medial side of the gallbladder using the 'open book' technique, enhancing visibility of the anatomy in the hepatocystic triangle. A small bleeder on the cystic artery is controlled, while the gallbladder is gradually pulled away from the liver.

The surgeon approaches the hepatocystic triangle, carefully staying close to the gallbladder to avoid the right hepatic artery and duct. The gallbladder is gently pulled away, enhancing the view of critical structures, similar to the traditional laparoscopic triangulation method.

The surgeon uses three instruments to achieve the critical view of safety, efficiently maneuvering the gallbladder away from critical structures. The gallbladder is pulled far from the liver and surrounding ducts, allowing easy access around the cystic duct without frequent instrument exchange.

The surgeon continues to work at a high position, avoiding the common hepatic artery and ducts, while dissecting more of the gallbladder using a hook. The critical view of safety has been achieved, and the surgeon inspects a structure to determine if it is a small bile duct, confirming it is not. The goal is to completely remove the gallbladder as the final clip is placed.

The surgeon continues to dissect the cystic duct, extending its length to ensure safe clipping and division with cautery, minimizing the risk of thermal injury. Most of the gallbladder has been detached, with the option to remove additional portions.

The surgeon leaves a portion of the gallbladder attached while preparing to apply a clip to the cystic duct. The clip applier is positioned carefully to avoid interference with the falciform ligament. Meanwhile, the surgeon uses hooks to continue detaching the gallbladder from the liver, maintaining continuous progress during the procedure.

The surgeon nearly completes the gallbladder dissection, while the bedside assistant quickly brings in the third clip. The clip applier is used as a liver retractor, and the cystic duct is divided using the cut function with good tension. Firefly imaging is utilized to check for bile leaks and ensure no small duct of Lushka is missed.

The surgeon swaps the hook for an endocatch bag, inserting it through the umbilical port using the Hassan technique. The gallbladder is placed into the endocatch for extraction. The clip applier is used as a grasper to manipulate the endocatch bag.
